content.text: What is the Azure REST API?

Every action you take in the Azure portal, az CLI, Az PowerShell module, or an Azure SDK ultimately calls the same underlying HTTP API: the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) REST API. Understanding this API lets you automate anything Azure exposes — including capabilities not yet wrapped by higher-level tools — using any HTTP client.

The Azure REST API follows the REST (Representational State Transfer) architectural style. Resources are addressed by URL, operations are expressed as HTTP methods (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE, PATCH), and data is exchanged in JSON. Every resource type has a specific API version baked into the URL, giving you stable, versioned access.

attrs.alt: Azure REST API request flow from client through Azure AD and ARM to a resource provider

content.text: Azure REST API URLs follow a consistent pattern. Understanding the URL tells you the subscription, resource group, resource type, resource name, and API version involved in any request.

https://management.azure.com
  /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}
  /resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}
  /providers/{resourceProvider}
  /{resourceType}
  /{resourceName}
  ?api-version={version}

Example — GET a virtual machine:
GET https://management.azure.com
    /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
    /resourceGroups/myRG
    /providers/Microsoft.Compute
    /virtualMachines
    /myVM
    ?api-version=2024-03-01

Example — List storage accounts in a subscription:
GET https://management.azure.com
    /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
    /providers/Microsoft.Storage
    /storageAccounts
    ?api-version=2023-05-01
Fixed; same for all ARM resources
Scope to a resource group (optional for list)

The api-version query parameter is mandatory on every Azure REST API call. Omitting it returns a 400 error. Use the latest stable version for new resources; check the Azure REST API reference docs for the current version of each resource type.
HTTP Methods and What They Do
Azure REST API operations map predictably to HTTP verbs. Knowing the verb tells you the semantics and whether the operation is idempotent.
Read a resource or list resources
Get VM details, list resource groups
Create or fully replace a resource
Create or update a storage account
Add a tag without replacing all properties
Start a VM, generate SAS token

# GET: list all resource groups
curl -s -X GET \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  jq '.value[].name'

# PUT: create a resource group
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"location": "eastus"}' \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myNewRG?api-version=2021-04-01"

# DELETE: delete a resource group (async)
curl -s -X DELETE \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myNewRG?api-version=2021-04-01"
# Returns 202 Accepted with Location header for polling

Every Azure REST API call requires an OAuth 2.0 bearer token issued by Azure AD (Microsoft Entra ID). The token proves that the caller is authenticated and carries the caller's identity for RBAC evaluation.

# Get a token for the ARM audience using az CLI
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
  --resource https://management.azure.com/ \
  --query accessToken \
  -o tsv)

SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Use in curl
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB?api-version=2022-12-01" | jq .displayName
Using a Service Principal (Client Credentials Flow)

# Client credentials flow: service principal authenticates with a secret
TENANT_ID=your-tenant-id
CLIENT_ID=your-sp-app-id
CLIENT_SECRET=your-sp-secret

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=$CLIENT_ID" \
  -d "client_secret=$CLIENT_SECRET" \
  -d "scope=https://management.azure.com/.default" \
  "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$TENANT_ID/oauth2/v2.0/token" | \
  jq -r '.access_token')

echo "Token obtained: ${TOKEN:0:20}..."

# On an Azure VM, App Service, or AKS pod with a managed identity:
# Call the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint — no credentials needed
TOKEN=$(curl -s -H 'Metadata: true' \
  'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2019-08-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/' | \
  jq -r '.access_token')

# For user-assigned managed identity, specify the client ID:
TOKEN=$(curl -s -H 'Metadata: true' \
  "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2019-08-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/&client_id=$MI_CLIENT_ID" | \
  jq -r '.access_token')
curl is the most direct way to explore and test the Azure REST API. It has no abstraction layer, so you see exactly what goes over the wire.

# Setup
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
RG=myRG

# List all VMs in a resource group
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2024-03-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name: .name, location: .location, size: .properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize}'

# Create a resource group with tags
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"location":"eastus","tags":{"env":"prod","team":"platform"}}' \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/newRG?api-version=2021-04-01"

# POST: restart a VM
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/restart?api-version=2024-03-01"
# Returns 202 Accepted — check Location header for operation status
Many Azure REST API operations are asynchronous — they return HTTP 202 Accepted immediately and complete in the background. You must poll a status URL to determine when the operation finishes.
Synchronous success; response body contains the result
Read body for the new resource
Async operation started; response includes Location or Azure-AsyncOperation header
Fix the request body or parameters
Resource already exists or is in a conflicting state
Rate limit hit; Retry-After header gives wait time
Back off and retry after the header value

# Poll an async operation until it completes
# Step 1: Initiate the async operation
RESPONSE=$(curl -si -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/start?api-version=2024-03-01")

# Step 2: Extract the polling URL from the response headers
POLL_URL=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | grep -i 'Location:' | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')
if [ -z "$POLL_URL" ]; then
  POLL_URL=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | grep -i 'Azure-AsyncOperation:' | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')
fi

# Step 3: Poll until succeeded or failed
while true; do
  STATUS=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "$POLL_URL" | jq -r '.status')
  echo "Status: $STATUS"
  case "$STATUS" in
    Succeeded) echo 'Operation completed successfully'; break ;;
    Failed|Canceled) echo 'Operation failed'; exit 1 ;;
    *) sleep 5 ;;
  esac
done
Using az rest for Direct API Calls

The az rest command lets you call any Azure REST API endpoint using your existing az login credentials, without manually acquiring a token. It handles authentication automatically and returns parsed JSON.

# az rest: GET request (auto-handles auth)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --query value[].name

# az rest: PUT request with body
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/newRG?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --body '{"location":"eastus","tags":{"env":"dev"}}'

# az rest: POST action on a resource
az rest \
  --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/deallocate?api-version=2024-03-01"

# az rest: use {subscriptionId} placeholder (auto-resolved)
# or pass --subscription to target a different subscription:
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --subscription 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000

az rest is the easiest way to explore REST API endpoints that the az CLI does not yet expose as named commands. The {subscriptionId} placeholder is automatically replaced with your current subscription ID.
The PowerShell equivalent of az rest is Invoke-AzRestMethod. It uses your current Az PowerShell session credentials and returns an object with StatusCode, Headers, and Content properties.

# GET: list resource groups
$sub = (Get-AzContext).Subscription.Id
$response = Invoke-AzRestMethod `
  -Method GET `
  -Path "/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"

if ($response.StatusCode -eq 200) {
    $body = $response.Content | ConvertFrom-Json
    $body.value | Select-Object name, location
}

# PUT: create a resource group
$body = @{ location = 'eastus'; tags = @{ env = 'prod' } } | ConvertTo-Json
$result = Invoke-AzRestMethod `
  -Method PUT `
  -Path "/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/newRG?api-version=2021-04-01" `
  -Payload $body
Write-Host "Status: $($result.StatusCode)"

# POST: start a VM
$vmResult = Invoke-AzRestMethod `
  -Method POST `
  -Path "/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/start?api-version=2024-03-01"
Write-Host "Async status: $($vmResult.StatusCode)"   # 202 for async operations
Azure REST API errors are returned as JSON with a structured error body. Always check the HTTP status code and parse the error body to diagnose failures.

# Capture both HTTP status and body for error handling
HTTP_RESPONSE=$(curl -s -w '\n%{http_code}' \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG?api-version=2021-04-01")

HTTP_BODY=$(echo "$HTTP_RESPONSE" | head -n -1)
HTTP_CODE=$(echo "$HTTP_RESPONSE" | tail -n 1)

if [ "$HTTP_CODE" -ge 200 ] && [ "$HTTP_CODE" -lt 300 ]; then
  echo "Success: $HTTP_CODE"
  echo "$HTTP_BODY" | jq '.name, .location'
elif [ "$HTTP_CODE" -eq 429 ]; then
  RETRY_AFTER=$(echo "$HTTP_RESPONSE" | grep -i 'Retry-After:' | awk '{print $2}')
  echo "Rate limited. Retry after ${RETRY_AFTER}s"
  sleep "${RETRY_AFTER:-10}"
else
  echo "Error $HTTP_CODE:"
  echo "$HTTP_BODY" | jq '.error'
fi
API Versioning and the Reference Documentation

Every resource type in Azure has its own API version timeline. New features are added in new API versions; old versions are deprecated but remain stable for a long time. Microsoft publishes the full reference documentation online.
Use the Azure REST API Browser (learn.microsoft.com/rest/api/azure/) to find the latest API version for any resource type.
API versions are dates (e.g., 2024-03-01) or dates with a preview suffix (e.g., 2024-07-01-preview). Prefer stable versions in production.
The az CLI shows the API version it uses: az vm show --debug 2>&1 | grep api-version
ARM templates and Bicep require explicit api-version on every resource block.
Each API version is independently stable; a new version never removes properties from the old version's response schema.

# Discover what API versions are available for a resource type
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].apiVersions[]" \
  -o tsv | head -10

# See which API version the Azure CLI uses for a given command
az vm list -g myRG --debug 2>&1 | grep -o 'api-version=[^&]*'

# List all registered resource providers in your subscription
az provider list --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].namespace" -o tsv | sort

Azure Resource Manager applies per-subscription and per-region rate limits to prevent runaway automation from degrading service for other users. Understanding these limits helps you write resilient code.

# Check remaining API quota from response headers
curl -si \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  grep -i 'x-ms-ratelimit'

# Example: retry with exponential backoff on 429
call_with_retry() {
  local url=$1 delay=2
  for attempt in 1 2 3 4; do
    HTTP_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "$url")
    if [ "$HTTP_CODE" -ne 429 ]; then
      return 0
    fi
    echo "Rate limited (attempt $attempt). Waiting ${delay}s..."
    sleep $delay
    delay=$((delay * 2))
  done
  echo 'All retries exhausted'
  return 1
}

call_with_retry "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"

Azure REST API list operations return paginated results. When a collection is too large to return in one response, the response body includes a nextLink property with a URL to fetch the next page. You must follow these links to get all results.

# Follow pagination to collect all resource groups
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

ALL_RGS=()
URL="https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"

while [ -n "$URL" ]; do
  RESPONSE=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "$URL")
  # Collect names from this page
  PAGE_NAMES=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.value[].name')
  while IFS= read -r name; do
    ALL_RGS+=("$name")
  done <<< "$PAGE_NAMES"
  # Get next page URL (empty string if last page)
  URL=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.nextLink // empty')
done

echo "Total resource groups: ${#ALL_RGS[@]}"
printf '%s\n' "${ALL_RGS[@]}"

# PowerShell: follow pagination automatically
$sub = (Get-AzContext).Subscription.Id
$allRGs = @()
$url = "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"

do {
    $response = Invoke-AzRestMethod -Method GET -Path $url.Replace('https://management.azure.com', '')
    $body = $response.Content | ConvertFrom-Json
    $allRGs += $body.value
    $url = $body.nextLink
} while ($url)

Write-Host "Total resource groups: $($allRGs.Count)"
$allRGs | Select-Object name, location | Sort-Object name
Filtering and Querying Results with OData

Some Azure REST API endpoints support OData query parameters for server-side filtering, sorting, and selecting fields. This reduces the amount of data transferred and speeds up queries against large collections.

# Filter resources by location using OData $filter
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01&\$filter=location+eq+'eastus'" | \
  jq '.value[] | .name'

# List only the first 5 resource groups ($top)
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01&\$top=5" | \
  jq '.value[] | .name'

# az rest equivalent with OData filter
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01&\$filter=location+eq+'eastus'"

Not all Azure REST API endpoints support OData filtering — support varies by resource type. Always check the API reference docs for the specific endpoint. When server-side filtering is unavailable, use jq or PowerShell Where-Object to filter client-side.
Working with ARM Resource Locks via REST API

Some advanced Azure operations — like applying or removing resource locks — are available via REST API before they were wrapped in higher-level tools. This is a practical example of using az rest to call an API that the CLI does not expose as a dedicated subcommand.

# List resource locks on a resource group
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks?api-version=2016-09-01"

# Apply a ReadOnly lock to prevent accidental changes
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks/PreventDelete?api-version=2016-09-01" \
  --body '{"properties":{"level":"CanNotDelete","notes":"Do not delete production RG"}}'

# Remove the lock
az rest \
  --method DELETE \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks/PreventDelete?api-version=2016-09-01"

# List all locks across the entire subscription
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks?api-version=2016-09-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name: .name, level: .properties.level, scope: .id}'
There are several ways to call Azure REST APIs, each with different tradeoffs in ease of use, control, and language portability.

# All three approaches for getting a resource group — side by side

# 1. az rest (simplest)
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG?api-version=2021-04-01"

# 2. curl with token (most transparent)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG?api-version=2021-04-01"

# 3. Python SDK (most robust in production)
python3 - << 'EOF'
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
import subprocess, json

sub_id = subprocess.check_output(['az','account','show','--query','id','-o','tsv']).decode().strip()
client = ResourceManagementClient(DefaultAzureCredential(), sub_id)
rg = client.resource_groups.get('myRG')
print(rg.name, rg.location)
EOF
Practical Example: Bulk Tag Update via REST API
Applying tags to many resources at once is a common operational task. The ARM REST API exposes a tag management endpoint that lets you merge tags without touching other resource properties.

#!/bin/bash
# Bulk apply tags to all resources in a resource group
set -euo pipefail

RG=myRG
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

NEW_TAGS='{"cost-center":"engineering","project":"alpha","env":"prod"}'

# Get all resource IDs in the resource group
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list --resource-group $RG --query '[].id' -o tsv)

while IFS= read -r RESOURCE_ID; do
  echo "Tagging: $RESOURCE_ID"
  az rest \
    --method PATCH \
    --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01" \
    --body "{\"operation\":\"Merge\",\"properties\":{\"tags\":$NEW_TAGS}}" \
    -o none
done <<< "$RESOURCE_IDS"

echo 'All resources tagged successfully'

The Azure REST API is the universal interface for all Azure management operations, expressed as HTTPS calls with JSON bodies to management.azure.com. Every URL encodes the subscription, resource group, resource provider, resource name, and a mandatory api-version. Operations are HTTP verbs: GET (read), PUT (create/replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove), and POST (custom actions). Authentication requires an OAuth 2.0 bearer token from Azure AD, obtainable via az account get-access-token, a service principal client-credentials flow, or a managed identity IMDS call. For quick exploration, use az rest or PowerShell's Invoke-AzRestMethod which handle authentication automatically. Long-running operations return HTTP 202 and require polling the Location or Azure-AsyncOperation header URL. Watch for 429 responses and implement exponential backoff to handle ARM throttling limits.
